coredata.dc:title: WS2 quantum dots as a sensitive fluorescence probe for the detection of glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Xinhe
Liu, Qing
Wang, Guannan
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a new fluorescence method was developed for glucose detection by using tungsten disulfide quantum dots (WS2 QDs) based on the transformation of Fe2+/Fe3+ and the enzymatic reaction. In the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence for WS2 QDs can be quenched via photoinduced electron transfer (PET), while Fe2+ does not influence the fluorescence of WS2 QDs. Glucose can produce hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in the presence of glucose oxidase (GOx). And Fe2+ can be transferred to Fe3+ via the oxidation of H2O2. So a sensitive and simple strategy for glucose detection can be developed due to the different influences of Fe2+ and Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs. Under the optimized experimental conditions, a linear response was obtained for glucose in the range of 1–60 μmol L−1 with a detection limit 0.30 μmol L−1. This method was further applied to the glucose detection in serum sample with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tungsten disulfide quantum dots
Glucose
Fe2+/Fe3+ redox
Fluorescence probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and construction of the sensing platform

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of WS2 QDs. The inset shows the size distribution of WS2 QDs. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra (red line) and fluorescence excitation (blue line) and emission (black line) spectra of WS2 QDs. The inset shows the corresponding photographs of WS2 QDs under the visible and UV light. (C) The fluorescence emission spectra of WS2 QDs under different excitation wavelengths in the range of 300–420 nm. (D) The FT-IR spectrum of WS2 QDs and GSH.

(A) The effect of different components on the fluorescence spectra of WS2 QDs. Concentration: 300 μmol L−1 Fe2+, 100 μmol L−1 H2O2, 100 μmol L−1 glucose, 300 μmol L−1 Fe3+, 4 μg mL−1 GOx. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra of WS2 QDs, Fe3+ and WS2 QDs/Fe3+ system.

(A) The effect of the concentration of Fe2+ on the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs/Fe2+ /H2O2 system. (B) The effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs/Fe2+/glucose /GOx system. (C) The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs, WS2 QDs/Fe2+, and WS2 QDs/Fe2+/glucose/GOx system. (D) The effect of the concentration of GOx on the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs/Fe2+/glucose/GOx system.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of WS2 QDs/Fe2+/GOx system with different concentration of glucose in the range of 0–60 μmol L−1(1, 4, 10, 12, 14, 22, 24, 26, 28, 34, 36, 40, 44, 50, 52, 54, 60 μmol L−1) Concentration: 300 μmol L−1 Fe2+, 4 μg mL−1 GOx (B) The relationship between quenching efficiency ((I0-I)/I0) and glucose concentration, where I0 and I were the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs/Fe2+/GOx system in the absence and presence of glucose.

The selectivity (A) and the interference (B) of the WS2 QDs/Fe2+ system for the detection of glucose. The concentration of glucose was 60 μmol L−1, and other interfering substances concentration were the same with glucose.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence detection for glucose based on WS2 QDs.
Determination of glucose in human serum.

head.ce:title: WS2 quantum dots as a sensitive fluorescence probe for the detection of glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry& The Key Laboratory for Medical Tissue Engineering of Liaoning Province, Jinzhou Medical University, Jinzhou 121001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry& The Key Laboratory for Medical Tissue Engineering of Liaoning Province, Jinzhou Medical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a new fluorescence method was developed for glucose detection by using tungsten disulfide quantum dots (WS2 QDs) based on the transformation of Fe2+/Fe3+ and the enzymatic reaction. In the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence for WS2 QDs can be quenched via photoinduced electron transfer (PET), while Fe2+ does not influence the fluorescence of WS2 QDs. Glucose can produce hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in the presence of glucose oxidase (GOx). And Fe2+ can be transferred to Fe3+ via the oxidation of H2O2. So a sensitive and simple strategy for glucose detection can be developed due to the different influences of Fe2+ and Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs. Under the optimized experimental conditions, a linear response was obtained for glucose in the range of 1–60 μmol L−1 with a detection limit 0.30 μmol L−1. This method was further applied to the glucose detection in serum sample with satisfactory results.

We developed a simple and convenient fluorescence probe to detect glucose based on the different influences of Fe2+ and Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs and the enzymatic reaction.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional materials have drawn great and significant attention in recent years, especially graphene due to its lower dimensionality [1]. However, other layered materials which are called inorganic analogues of graphene (IAG) also should be noticed [2]. As one kind of the typical IAG, WS2 has shown its applications as catalyst [3], lubricants [4], batteries [5] and photonic devices [6]. If bulk WS2 is exfoliated to nanosheets, the band gap will be changed [7], which makes WS2 nanosheets possess excellent properties and show promising applications, such as biological detection [8], electrocatalyst [9,10], sensor [11] and so on. However, WS2 nanosheets have some limitations because of its low quality, inadequate active sites. In order to overcome these drawbacks, the major focus of research is towards the application of WS2 QDs. Excellent luminous properties and high biocompatibility have been observed in WS2 QDs, and these properties provide it promising prospects to bioanalysis applications [12–14]. However, the detection of biomolecules using WS2 QDs is still rather scarce. Furthermore, there is no report on the application of WS2 QDs in the fluorescence detection of glucose.

Glucose plays an important role in biology, which is the only carbohydrate in serum [15]. And the concentration of glucose is closely related to the health of people. On the one hand, the lack of glucose may cause hypoglycemia. On the other hand, superabundant glucose may lead to some diseases, such as diabetes, hypertension and cardiovascular diseases [16]. Until now, many analytical approaches have been reported to detect glucose including electrochemical [17], colorimetric method [18] and mass spectrometry [19]. However, electrochemical method are easily influenced by electroactive species and sample matrixes [15], colorimetric analysis has poor reproducibility, mass spectrometry require high experimental cost and complicated instrument. Compared with these methods, the fluorescence approach has attracted increasing attention due to its high selectivity, rapid response and simplicity. Moreover, the fluorescence method do not require high experimental cost and complicated procedures, therefore, a variety of methods have been fabricated based on fluorescent compounds to detect glucose. Li et al. designed a perylene probe, which can be quenched by the silver nanoparticles. And the silver nanoparticles could be etched by H2O2 generated from the oxidation of glucose in the presence of GOx. Then the perylene probe fluorescence would be recovery [20]. Lin et al. developed a sensitive fluorescence “turn-on” strategy for measuring glucose by using the label-free nanozyme MIL-53(Fe) [21]. Vaishanav et al. described a highly sensitive Mn2+-doped-CdTe/ZnS modified nanosensor for detecting glucose on the basis of electron transfer [22]. However, these previous methods also suffered from complicated modification, expensive reagents and laborious procedures. Therefore, it is still worthy to develope a convenience, inexpensive and sensitive method to detect glucose.

In this paper, we constructed an optical sensing strategy to detect glucose according to the redox reactions between various valence state of iron. The mechanism was illustrated in Scheme 1. Glucose can generate H2O2 in the presence of GOx and Fe2+ could be oxidated by H2O2 to Fe3+. Fe3+ can quench the fluorescence of WS2 QDs via PET, while Fe2+ does not influence the fluorescence of WS2 QDs. So a fluorescence approach to detect glucose can be discovered on the basis of different influences of Fe2+ and Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs.

All reagents were of at least analytical grade. The water used in all experiments had a resistivity higher than 18 MΩ cm−1·H2O2 (30%), HCl (36%), urea, glucose, L-threonine, L-histidine, L-alanine, L-phenylalanine, L-arginine, L-glycine, L-glutamic acid were obtained from Beijing Dingguo Biotechnology Co. Ltd. Sodium tungstate (Na2WO4·2H2O), reduced L-glutathione (GSH), FeCl2, FeCl3, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2 and glucose oxidase (GOx) were purchased from Huacheng Biological Co. Ltd (Changchun).

All fluorescence spectra were measured by a RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). FT-IR speca was recorded by using a Bruker IFS66V FT-IR spectrometer. UV–Vis spectrum was obtained with an UV–Vis spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained using a Hitachi H-800 electron microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV with a CCD camera.

WS2 QDs were synthesized according to a reported method with some modifications [12]. Firstly, sodium tungstate (0.0125 g) was dissolved in 2.5 mL deionized water, and the pH was adjusted to 6.0 by adding 0.1 M HCL. Then this mixture and 0.1725 g reduced GSH were transferred to a beaker which was containing 10 mL water. After that, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. At last, the reactor was placed in the oven and heated at 200 °C for 9 h. The WS2 QDs then was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for use.

For the detection of glucose, various concentrations of glucose were mixed with GOx solution (4 μg mL−1, buffered with 10 mmol L−1Tris-HCl, pH 6.2) in 37°C for 40 min. After that, Fe2+ (300 μmol L−1) and 20 μL WS2 QDS were added. The mixture was shaken at room temperature for 5 min before fluorescent measurement.

The blood samples were provided by the university hospital. All the blood sample were mixed with acetonitrile to remove protein and the product would be vigorously shaked for 2 min. Then the blood samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was stored in −20°C until it was used. Deionized water was added to the human serum for the purpose of diluting the serum and different glucose concentrations were added to prerare spiked samples. Real sample detection was followed by the steps described above.

In this study, sodium tungstate was selected as the tungsten source for hydrothermal synthesis of WS2 QDs, while GSH was adopted as the sulfur source. The fluorescent intensity of WS2 QDs was influenced by several factors, such as the ratio between the sulfur source and the tungsten source, the reaction time and the pH. Thus, we optimized the synthesis conditions in order to obtain WS2 QDs with good fluorescence properties. According to Fig. S1, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs reached maximum when reaction time was 9 h. So the optimal reaction time of 9 h was chosen. Fig. S2 showed that the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs at pH 6.0 was maximum, and pH 6.0 was selected for WS2 QDs synthesis. Fig. S3 illustrated the effect of the ratio of the tungsten source and the sulfur source on the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs. When the ratio of sulfur source to tungsten source was 15, the fluorescent intensity of WS2 QDs was strong. The reason may be excess sulfur would block the passivated surface defects, leading to the fluorescent intensity decreased [23]. Therefore, the optimal ratio of sulfur source to tungsten source was chosen 15 in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and construction of the sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The lateral size of WS2 QDs could be obtained by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image. Fig. 1A exhibited the TEM images of WS2 QDs, it can be seen that WS2 QDs showed good dispersion in aqueous and the average diameter of WS2 QDs was 2.85 nm. The UV–vis spectra of WS2 QDs was displayed in Fig. 1B the absorption peaks of 320 nm and 395 nm were the two excitonic absorption peaks of WS2 QDs. The appearance of 280 nm peak may be due to the optical transitions, which happened between the density of state peaks in the valence and conduction band [24,25]. Fig. 1B also showed the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of WS2 QDs. There existed a strong and wide absorption peak between 300 nm and 400 nm from the excitation spectrum. It can be seen from the emission spectrum, the maximum emission wavelength of WS2 QDs is at 430 nm. The inset of Fig. 1B shows the color of WS2 QDS under the visible and UV light was light yellow and blue, respectively. Fig. 1C showed the fluorescence spectra of WS2 QDs was nearly centered at 430 nm at different excitation wavelengths, which indicating its emission wavelength was independence of excitation wavelength. The fluorescence intensity reached maximum, when the excitation wavelength is 320 nm. Therefore, the excitation wavelength of 320 nm was chosen in the further experiments. Fig. 1D shows the FT-IR spectra of WS2 QDs and GSH. It can be seen that there are four major peaks of WS2 QDs. The peak at 3100 cm−1 was assigned O-H stretching band and another hydroxyl's characteristic peaks was at 1350 cm−1, which was the deformation peak from hydroxyl. Also the peak at 1700 cm−1, 1200 cm−1 were ascribed to the C˭O stretching band and the C-O stretching band. After the formation of WS2 QDs, it was noted that the characteristic peak at 2500 cm−1 of GSH had disappeared, which was corresponding to the S-H stretching band. Meanwhile, the peak at 1600 cm−1 was N-H deformation peak and the intensity is decreased. Based on the above results, we confirmed the formation of WS2 QDs.

Fig. 2A showed the effect of different components on the fluorescence spectra of WS2 QDS. As can be seen from Fig. 2A, the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs can be remarkably quenched by Fe3+ or the mixture of H2O2 and Fe2+. H2O2 and Fe2+ had little influence on the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs /Fe2+ system had no evident change in the presence of glucose or GOx. However, when glucose and GOx were coexisting, the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs /Fe2+ system was quenched a lot. This phenomenon proved that Fe2+ can be transferred to Fe3+ with H2O2, generated by the catalytic oxidation of glucose by GOx, and further quench the fluorescence of WS2 QDs.

Then, we studied the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The quenching phenomenon caused by the metal ions is usually divided into static quenching and dynamic quenching. We can distinguish it according to the relationship between the quenching constants and temperature [26,27]. In this work, we used Stern-Volmer equation, F0/F= 1 +Ksv[Q] to identify the quenching behavior of Fe3+ on the fluorescence of WS2 QDs (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of WS2 QDs with and without the quencher Fe3+, respectively; [Q] represents the concentration of the Fe3+; Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant). The equation mentioned above was applied to calculate Ksv of the WS2 QDs/Fe3+ system at three different temperatures (shown in Fig. S4). The values of Ksv were summarized in Table S1. It can be seen that the Ksv was decreased with the increase of temperature, indicating that the quenching process of WS2 QDs/Fe3+ system is static quenching. Furthermore, the UV–vis absorption spectra of WS2 QDs, Fe3+, and WS2 QDs/Fe3+ system were investigated. It can be seen from Fig. 2B, the UV–vis absorption spectra of WS2 QDs was obviously changed upon the addition of Fe3+ compared to that of WS2 QDs or Fe3+, confirming the formation of new substance between WS2 QDs and Fe3+, therefore this quenching type was static quenching [28], and the static quenching may be caused by photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Previous reports indicated that Fe3+ ions can coordinate with carboxyl groups [29,30]. FT-IR proved that the presence of abundant surface carboxyl group on the surface of WS2 QDs. Fe3+ was able to absorb on the surface of WS2 QDs and coordinate with carboxyl groups. Due to this coordination interaction, electrons in the excited state of WS2 QDs would transfer to the unfilled orbital of Fe3+, which led to the fluorescence quenching of WS2 QDs through photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process.

In this study, The optimal experimental conditions including reaction time, pH and the concentration of Fe2+, glucose oxidase for glucose detection were studied. We first studied the effect of Fe2+ concentration. Fig. 3A illustrated that the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs/H2O2 system declined sharply when Fe2+ concentration increased. And it kept stable after the concentration of Fe2+ was 300 μmol L−1. So 300 μmol L−1 of Fe2+ was selected in the further experiments. Then, we investigated the effect of reaction time, pH and the concentration of GOx on glucose detection. Fig. 3B showed that the fluorescence intensity of sensing system declined as the reaction time increased. And there is no obvious change after 35 min. So 40 min was selected as the reaction time. The influence of pH on the sensing system of WS2 QDs for glucose detection was studied, as shown in Fig. 3C. It was evident that pH had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs and WS2 QDs/Fe2+ from pH 5.5 to 7.4, and the differences of the fluorescence intensity between WS2 QDs and WS2 QDs/Fe2+ /glucose /GOx system was the largest at pH 6.2. So we chose pH 6.2 as selected for further glucose detection. It can be seen from Fig. 3D that the fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly with the increase of GOx concentration, and remained stable when GOx concentration was more than 4 μg mL−1. Therefore, we selected 4 μg mL−1 GOx for following experiments.

Fig. 4A showed the fluorescence emission spectra of WS2 QDS/Fe2+/GOx system with different concentration of glucose under the optimum conditions. As shown in Fig. 4A, when different concentrations of glucose were added into the WS2 QDS/Fe2+/GOx system, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the increase of glucose concentrations, and the linear range was obtained in the range of 1–60 μmol L−1. The relationship between relative fluorescence intensity (I0-I)/I0 and the concentration of glucose was shown in Fig. 4B, which can be expressed as (I0-I)/I0 =0.0117Cglucose-0.0019, μmol L−1, with a corresponding regression coefficient of 0.9994. The detection limit (LOD)

for glucose detection was 0.30 μmol L−1. The detection limit was defined by the equation LOD = 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signals and s is the slope of the calibration curve. The repeatability of this method for glucose detection was evaluated by measuring the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 6 μmol L−1 glucose in five replicates, and the RSD was 2.8%. A comparison data including the detection limit, linear range, reliability of data, as well as the convenience of the detection process have been given (Table S2). Compared with other reported methods, our method offered a comparable or superior LOD, linear range, RSD and reaction time. We also evaluated the detection results calculated from measuring the absorption of the solution to show the superiorities of the reported system using the emission of WS2 as the signal. It can be seen from Fig. S5, the absorption of the solution at 300 nm increased with increasing the concentration of glucose over the concentration range from 40 μmol L−1 to 200 μmol L−1 and the linear range was obtained in the range of 50–100 μmol L−1. The above results proved that our fluorescence method by is more sensitive.

The selectivity and interference study are both important to evaluate the feasibility of this method. In this study, some metal ions (NaCl, MgCl2, MnCl2, CaCl2), glucose analogues (D-galactose, lactose, fructose) and amino acids (L-threonine, L-arginine, L-glycine, L-glutamic acid and L-histidine) were used as substances. The same concentrations of interferences were added into WS2 QDs/Fe2+/GOx system. Fig. 5A displayed that fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs/Fe3+/GOx system were not apparently influenced when interferences existed in the solution except glucose. Furthermore, we investigated the influences of interference substances to the WS2 QDs/Fe3+/GOx system with 60 μmol L−1 of glucose. Fig. 5B demonstrated that these interference substances also had no evident interferences on the glucose detection, indicating the established WS2 QDs/Fe3+/GOx system displayed well anti-interference ability for glucose detection.

In order to investigate the application of our method in real sample, The method was applied for glucose detection in human serum. The results obtained by the standard addition method were listed in Table 1, it can be seen that the average recovery of glucose was between 97.7% and 102.5% and RSD was lower than 3%. The aboved results indicated the potential applicability of WS2 QDs/Fe2+/GOx system for the glucose detection in real sample.

In general, we established a convenience and sensitive approach to detect glucose on the basis of different quenching abilities of Fe2+, Fe3+ and enzymatic reaction. It could be used for the detection of glucose in the range of 1–60 μmol L−1, with a detection limit of 0.30 μmol L−1. Furthermore, the strategy for the detection of glucose was successfully used in real samples. Due to its simple and sensitivity, this sensing method shows great potential applications in clinical medicine examination and disease diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575048, No. 21775052 and No. 81671742), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20180414013GH).

ce:section.ce:para: There was no conflict of Interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.11.034.
